REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Most necessary details are provided: hyperparameters are reported, the UD v1.2 dataset is specified (they note excluding Japan-KTC and using the first treebank per language, concatenating up to 10,000 tokens per source), and evaluation metrics (averaged accuracy/attachment score) and baselines are described. However, code is not mentioned and some implementation specifics are likely missing (exact manual priors, full blocked-Gibbs sampling settings, and any parser-specific configuration or random seeds), so reimplementation is feasible but would require minor assumptions. | B(4): Most experimental details are reported in the summary: hyperparameters are stated as reported, the preprocessing choices are described (UD v1.2, 32 treebanks, exclude Japan-KTC, use first treebank where multiple, cap sources at 10,000 tokens), and the evaluation setup (leave-one-out multi-source transfer, averaged accuracy/attachment score) is specified. The dataset is a public collection (Universal Dependencies v1.2). However, code is not mentioned and some implementation-level details (exact priors, sampling settings/SEEDs, and parser implementations/tuning) may be missing, so full replication would require contacting authors or reimplementing parts from the description.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses core NLP research (cross-lingual/delexicalized transfer parsing and minimally supervised unsupervised parsing) that is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML conferences. It uses community-standard datasets and evaluation (Universal Dependencies v1.2, leave-one-out multi-source transfer, MST/Malt parsers, averaged attachment/accuracy) and compares to relevant prior work (McDonald et al. 2011; Rosa 2015), matching expected methodological and evaluation norms. The problem formulation, baselines, and positioning (thorough related work coverage) target the correct audience and venue community. | B(5): The paper addresses cross-lingual dependency parsing and minimally supervised unsupervised parsing using standard datasets (Universal Dependencies v1.2), parsers (MST, Malt) and evaluation metrics (averaged attachment accuracy), which squarely falls within top-tier NLP/ML conference scope. It compares against established baselines (McDonald et al. 2011; Rosa 2015) and uses leave-one-out multi-source transfer, blocked Gibbs inference, and thorough related-work and hyperparameter reporting, matching community expectations for methods and evaluation. The topic, audience, and problem formulation (delexicalized transfer baselines and minimally supervised UDP with external priors) are appropriate and well positioned for a flagship venue.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—cross-lingual and low-resource dependency parsing—and demonstrates that annotation unification (UD v1.2) yields substantially higher averaged accuracy (70.1% vs. 61.7% from McDonald et al., 2011) and better averages than Rosa (2015) on 23 languages (62.5% vs. 56.6%), which makes the UD-based delexicalized multi-source transfer a strong, widely applicable baseline. Its minimally supervised unsupervised parser, augmented with manual external priors, is competitive and notably outperforms the transfer parser on many non-Indo-European (low-resource) languages, indicating useful implications for under-resourced languages and for combining priors with unsupervised methods. However, because the unsupervised approach still has a slightly lower overall average attachment score than delexicalized transfer and the work is primarily empirical benchmarking on UD v1.2, the contribution is useful and likely to be built upon but not transformative for the field. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—cross-lingual dependency parsing for low-resource languages—and demonstrates substantial empirical gains from annotation unification: delexicalized multi-source transfer on UD v1.2 yields much higher averaged accuracy than prior work (70.1% vs. 61.7% reported by McDonald et al., 2011, and 62.5% vs. 56.6% vs. Rosa (2015) on 23 languages). The minimally supervised unsupervised parser augmented with manual external priors is particularly significant because it outperforms the transfer parser on many non-Indo-European (low-resourced) languages, indicating practical value for under-resourced scenarios. By establishing a strong UD-based multi-source delexicalized baseline and showing where minimally supervised methods help, the work is likely to influence experimental practice and open follow-up research directions, though its implications are mainly within multilingual dependency parsing rather than broadly transformative across NLP.

The minimally supervised unsupervised parser augmented with manual external priors is particularly significant because it outperforms the transfer parser on many non-Indo-European (low-resourced) languages, indicating practical value for under-resourced scenarios. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Based on the summary, the paper is generally well organized (intro → methods detailing multi-source delexicalized transfer and the minimally supervised UDP, experiments on UD v1.2, and conclusions) and states its main contributions early (improvements from UD unification and the augmented unsupervised parser). Technical presentation appears clear where noted (hyperparameters reported, thorough related-work coverage), and overall writing quality is described as adequate. However, the summary does not mention figures/tables or explicit notation definitions, so minor presentation details may be missing. | B(4): The paper appears well organized (intro → methods describing delexicalized transfer and minimally supervised UDP → experiments on UD v1.2 → conclusions) and states its main contributions early (improvements from UD annotation unification and the minimally supervised parser’s behavior). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters and datasets) are reported, suggesting definitions are consistent, and results are clearly compared to prior baselines (McDonald et al., Rosa). One minor presentation gap is that figures/tables/examples are not explicitly mentioned in the summary, and the writing is described only as “adequate,” indicating small clarity or presentation improvements could be made.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experiments cover multi-source delexicalized transfer on UD v1.2 (leave-one-out concatenating up to 10k tokens per source) with MST and Malt, and evaluate a minimally supervised UDP augmented with external priors, comparing to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015); hyperparameters are reported and related work is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of key choices (e.g., priors, token-cap, parser variants), code is not mentioned, and the corpus setup is somewhat restricted (32 treebanks, first treebank per language, Japan-KTC excluded), limiting reproducibility and comprehensiveness. Overall the evaluation is adequate but misses important analyses that would strengthen the claims. | B(4): The experiments are solid and fairly comprehensive: multi-source leave-one-out delexicalized transfer was run on the full UD v1.2 collection (32 treebanks) with two parser families (MST, Malt) and results are compared to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015), and a minimally supervised unsupervised parser variant (with manual and learned external priors) is evaluated and analyzed per-language. Hyperparameters and dataset selection (first treebank per language, up to 10k tokens per source) are reported, and limitations (unsupervised approach slightly worse on average but better on many non‑Indo-European languages) are discussed. Missing, however, are explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of token limit per source, prior choices) and code release is not mentioned, which somewhat reduces reproducibility and depth of analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover multi-source delexicalized transfer on UD v1.2 (leave-one-out concatenating up to 10k tokens per source) with MST and Malt, and evaluate a minimally supervised UDP augmented with external priors, comparing to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015); hyperparameters are reported and related work is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of key choices (e.g., priors, token-cap, parser variants), code is not mentioned, and the corpus setup is somewhat restricted (32 treebanks, first treebank per language, Japan-KTC excluded), limiting reproducibility and comprehensiveness. Overall the evaluation is adequate but misses important analyses that would strengthen the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid and fairly comprehensive: multi-source leave-one-out delexicalized transfer was run on the full UD v1.2 collection (32 treebanks) with two parser families (MST, Malt) and results are compared to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015), and a minimally supervised unsupervised parser variant (with manual and learned external priors) is evaluated and analyzed per-language. Hyperparameters and dataset selection (first treebank per language, up to 10k tokens per source) are reported, and limitations (unsupervised approach slightly worse on average but better on many non‑Indo-European languages) are discussed. Missing, however, are explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of token limit per source, prior choices) and code release is not mentioned, which somewhat reduces reproducibility and depth of analysis.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main empirical claims are supported by experiments comparing delexicalized multi-source transfer on UD v1.2 to published baselines (reporting 70.1% vs. 61.7% and 62.5% vs. 56.6%), and the minimally supervised UDP variant is evaluated and shown to outperform transfer on many low-resource non-Indo-European languages. The methodology uses sensible baselines (MST, Malt, prior published results) and a clear leave-one-out multi-source setup on the UD collection with reported hyperparameters and inference (blocked Gibbs). Minor technical concerns remain: the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, and dataset choices (truncating to 10k tokens per source, using only the first treebank where multiple exist, and excluding Japan-KTC) could affect generality; details on how manual priors were set/validated are also limited. Overall the evidence and methodology largely support the claims, with a few minor issues that do not overturn the conclusions. | B(4): The main claims are backed by concrete experimental comparisons (e.g., averaged accuracy 70.1% on UD v1.2 vs. 61.7% reported by McDonald et al., 2011 and 62.5% vs. 56.6% vs. Rosa (2015)), and the methodology (leave-one-out multi-source transfer on UD v1.2, concatenating up to 10k tokens per source, using MST and Malt baselines, and a minimally supervised UDP with external priors and blocked Gibbs sampling) is appropriate. Mathematical content appears limited and correct as described; hyperparameters are reported and relevant baselines are used. However, the summary does not report statistical significance testing and the choice to use the first treebank per language and truncate sources to 10k tokens could affect generality, hence minor methodological concerns. Overall the evidence supports the claims notwithstanding these limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The main empirical claims are supported by experiments comparing delexicalized multi-source transfer on UD v1.2 to published baselines (reporting 70.1% vs. 61.7% and 62.5% vs. 56.6%), and the minimally supervised UDP variant is evaluated and shown to outperform transfer on many low-resource non-Indo-European languages. The methodology uses sensible baselines (MST, Malt, prior published results) and a clear leave-one-out multi-source setup on the UD collection with reported hyperparameters and inference (blocked Gibbs). Minor technical concerns remain: the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, and dataset choices (truncating to 10k tokens per source, using only the first treebank where multiple exist, and excluding Japan-KTC) could affect generality; details on how manual priors were set/validated are also limited. Overall the evidence and methodology largely support the claims, with a few minor issues that do not overturn the conclusions.

However, the summary does not report statistical significance testing and the choice to use the first treebank per language and truncate sources to 10k tokens could affect generality, hence minor methodological concerns. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper mainly presents empirical findings (that UD v1.2 annotation unification improves multi-source delexicalized transfer, e.g. 70.1% vs. 61.7%) and applies established parsers (MST, Malt) in a leave-one-out UD setup, which is a well-studied problem rather than a new formulation. The augmentation of an existing unsupervised parser (UDP) with manually assigned external priors for attach/stop probabilities and blocked Gibbs sampling is a modest, practical extension that yields competitive results but is not a fundamentally new method. Contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work and useful, but overall the techniques and problem framing are incremental rather than highly novel. | B(3): The paper largely applies established approaches (multi-source delexicalized transfer with MST and Malt) to the Universal Dependencies v1.2 collection and shows that UD annotation unification yields improved averaged accuracy versus prior reports (McDonald et al., 2011; Rosa, 2015), which is a useful empirical finding but not a fundamentally new method. The minimally supervised variant—augmenting the Mareček & Straka (2013) unsupervised parser with manually assigned external priors for attach/stop probabilities and using blocked Gibbs sampling—is a modest, non-trivial tweak that improves performance on some low-resource languages but does not introduce a wholly new modeling paradigm. The paper clearly differentiates its contributions and provides informative results, so its novelty is moderate and best characterized as an incremental but well-motivated advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper mainly presents empirical findings (that UD v1.2 annotation unification improves multi-source delexicalized transfer, e.g. 70.1% vs. 61.7%) and applies established parsers (MST, Malt) in a leave-one-out UD setup, which is a well-studied problem rather than a new formulation. The augmentation of an existing unsupervised parser (UDP) with manually assigned external priors for attach/stop probabilities and blocked Gibbs sampling is a modest, practical extension that yields competitive results but is not a fundamentally new method. Contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work and useful, but overall the techniques and problem framing are incremental rather than highly novel.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper largely applies established approaches (multi-source delexicalized transfer with MST and Malt) to the Universal Dependencies v1.2 collection and shows that UD annotation unification yields improved averaged accuracy versus prior reports (McDonald et al., 2011; Rosa, 2015), which is a useful empirical finding but not a fundamentally new method. The minimally supervised variant—augmenting the Mareček & Straka (2013) unsupervised parser with manually assigned external priors for attach/stop probabilities and using blocked Gibbs sampling—is a modest, non-trivial tweak that improves performance on some low-resource languages but does not introduce a wholly new modeling paradigm. The paper clearly differentiates its contributions and provides informative results, so its novelty is moderate and best characterized as an incremental but well-motivated advance.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against standard delexicalized transfer baselines (MST and Malt) and cites quantitative results from McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015), reporting comparisons on shared languages and common subsets of UD — using the same evaluation metrics (averaged accuracy/attachment score). It acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better (the minimally supervised parser has slightly lower overall average but beats transfer on many low-resource languages) and discusses differences between methods. Minor omissions include lack of mention of very recent neural cross-lingual baselines, but overall the comparisons are good and fair given the reported setup. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate baselines (MST and Malt delexicalized transfer) and directly reports comparisons to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015) on shared/common languages. Comparisons appear fair—using leave-one-out on UD v1.2 and reporting averaged accuracy/attachment scores on common language sets—and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better (the minimally supervised parser has slightly lower average attachment but outperforms on many non-Indo-European languages). The summary also notes thorough related-work coverage and language-level analysis, supporting a comprehensive and fair evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against standard delexicalized transfer baselines (MST and Malt) and cites quantitative results from McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015), reporting comparisons on shared languages and common subsets of UD — using the same evaluation metrics (averaged accuracy/attachment score). It acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better (the minimally supervised parser has slightly lower overall average but beats transfer on many low-resource languages) and discusses differences between methods. Minor omissions include lack of mention of very recent neural cross-lingual baselines, but overall the comparisons are good and fair given the reported setup.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate baselines (MST and Malt delexicalized transfer) and directly reports comparisons to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015) on shared/common languages. Comparisons appear fair—using leave-one-out on UD v1.2 and reporting averaged accuracy/attachment scores on common language sets—and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better (the minimally supervised parser has slightly lower average attachment but outperforms on many non-Indo-European languages). The summary also notes thorough related-work coverage and language-level analysis, supporting a comprehensive and fair evaluation.

main_claims: The Universal Dependencies (v1.2) annotation unification improves delexicalized multi-source transfer parsing, yielding higher averaged accuracy on shared languages (70.1% on UD vs. 61.7% reported by McDonald et al., 2011) and higher average scores than Rosa (2015) on 23 common languages (62.5% vs. 56.6%).

A minimally supervised unsupervised dependency parser augmented with manually assigned external priors for attach and stop probabilities achieves competitive performance: its average attachment score is slightly lower than the delexicalized transfer parser overall, but it outperforms the transfer parser on many non-Indo-European (low-resourced) languages.

Multi-source delexicalized transfer (leave-one-out) trained on concatenated UD treebanks (taking first 10,000 tokens per language) using MST and Malt parsers is an effective baseline for cross-lingual parsing on the UD collection.
_summary.methodology: They perform multi-source delexicalized transfer parsing in a leave-one-out setup on Universal Dependencies v1.2 (concatenating up to 10,000 tokens per source language) using Malt and MST parsers, and they run an unsupervised dependency parser (UDP by Mareček & Straka 2013) enhanced with manually assigned (and also learned) external priors for pextstop and pextattach; inference for the UDP is done with blocked Gibbs sampling.
datasets: Universal Dependencies (UD) treebank collection v1.2 (32 dependency treebanks; excluded Japan-KTC; for languages with multiple treebanks they used the first one)
baselines: Results reported in McDonald et al. (2011)
limitations: The minimally supervised parser has a slightly lower average attachment score than the delexicalized transfer parser.
The authors note that unsupervised dependency parsing research has declined because results do not yet compete with projection and delexicalized methods.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dependency parsing (cross-lingual delexicalized transfer and unsupervised/minimally supervised parsing)
